subject: Chemistry
title: Hennig Brand and the Glowing Urine
description: A quick, practical guide to Hennig Brand and the Glowing Urine.

hook.text: In 1669, Hennig Brand boiled 1,500 gallons of urine looking for gold. He found a new element instead—phosphorus.

buildup.text: Brand was a bankrupt German merchant who believed urine's golden color meant it contained gold.

discovery.text: After months of boiling and distilling, he produced a waxy substance that glowed in the dark—phosphorus.

twist.text: He tried to keep the discovery secret and sell it. But Robert Boyle independently reproduced it and published the method.

climax.text: Phosphorus became essential for matches, fertilizers, and explosives. Brand's disgusting experiment changed the world.

punchline.text: 1,500 gallons of urine, no gold. Instead, the first new element in centuries.

quiz.question: How did Hennig Brand discover phosphorus?
options: By boiling and distilling large quantities of urine
By heating gold ore in a new type of furnace
By mixing acids with ground bones
